mechanism: Both block serotonin reuptake/metabolism causing dangerous synaptic serotonin accumulation
resulting_harm: Serotonin Syndrome: cognitive dysfunction + autonomic instability + somatic tremors/hyperthermia/seizures/multiorgan failure
notes: 14-day washout required when switching; 5 weeks for fluoxetine

mechanism: Dual serotonin reuptake inhibition from TCA+MAOI
resulting_harm: Serotonin Syndrome
notes: Only imipramine and clomipramine have serotonin potency; nortriptyline/desipramine are safer with MAOIs

mechanism: Combined serotonergic action via multiple pathways — risk increases significantly with third serotonergic agent
resulting_harm: Serotonin Syndrome
notes: Risk especially high when a triptan is added as third serotonergic agent

mechanism: Linezolid acts as weak MAO-A inhibitor (Ki=55µM) combined with serotonergic drug
resulting_harm: Serotonin Syndrome (mild to severe)
notes: Rare but documented; linezolid's weak MAO inhibition is sufficient to trigger in combination

mechanism: Combined serotonergic activity from herbal MAO inhibition + SSRI
resulting_harm: Serotonin Syndrome
notes: OTC herbal with documented serious interaction — patients often unaware

mechanism: Synergistic CNS depression: opioids act on mu-receptors (respiratory control) + benzodiazepines potentiate GABAA (CNS depression)
resulting_harm: Respiratory depression / extreme sedation / coma / death
notes: FDA 2016 Black Box Warning; observational studies confirm increased mortality vs opioids alone

mechanism: Synergistic CNS and respiratory depression at multiple receptor sites
resulting_harm: Respiratory depression / overdose death / impaired cognition
notes: Strong warning in all opioid labeling; major cause of opioid overdose deaths

mechanism: MAOI inhibits MAO-A (blocks NE breakdown); amphetamine blocks reuptake AND releases NE — synergistic synaptic NE accumulation
resulting_harm: Hypertensive crisis / stroke / myocardial infarction
notes: 14-day washout required; therapeutic combination sometimes used under careful monitoring

mechanism: NE reuptake blockade + MAOI NE preservation → alpha-1 receptor overstimulation
resulting_harm: Hypertensive crisis / myocardial infarction
notes: Absolute contraindication; ADHD patients on stimulants must not take MAOIs

mechanism: Direct alpha-1 agonist + MAOI-preserved NE → excessive arteriole resistance
resulting_harm: Hypertensive crisis / acute coronary syndrome
notes: OTC decongestant; FDA warns against concurrent use; patients on MAOIs should avoid ALL decongestants

mechanism: Sympathomimetic activity + MAOI effect
resulting_harm: Hypertensive crisis
notes: OTC cold medicine; patients on MAOIs must avoid

mechanism: TMP-SMX inhibits CYP2C9 → decreases clearance of S-warfarin (5x more potent isomer)
resulting_harm: Excessive anticoagulation / hemorrhage
notes: INR prolonged by mean 12.6s; close INR monitoring required

mechanism: CYP2C9 inhibition increases warfarin plasma levels
resulting_harm: Increased bleeding risk / hemorrhage
notes: Common antibiotic + common anticoagulant; dangerous combination

mechanism: Combined antiplatelet (COX-1 inhibition) + anticoagulation → additive bleeding
resulting_harm: Bleeding increased 2–4 fold / GI hemorrhage / intracranial hemorrhage
notes: Even low-dose aspirin significantly increases risk with warfarin

mechanism: COX-1 inhibition + CYP2C9 inhibition increases warfarin levels
resulting_harm: Major bleeding (GI / CNS / urinary) — 1.79x baseline risk
notes: Higher risk with naproxen (4.1x) and diclofenac (3.3x)

mechanism: CYP450 induction increases warfarin clearance → subtherapeutic anticoagulation
resulting_harm: Loss of anticoagulation / thrombosis / stroke / DVT
notes: Anticonvulsant commonly used long-term; opposite direction of interaction (reduces warfarin effect)

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition increases citalopram levels; both agents prolong QT via hERG channel blockade
resulting_harm: QT prolongation / Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic ventricular tachycardia)
notes: Risk enhanced by hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia; female sex; age >65

mechanism: Dual QT-prolonging agents via hERG potassium channel blockade
resulting_harm: QT prolongation / Torsades de Pointes
notes: Both very commonly prescribed; dangerous combination; QTc baseline needed

mechanism: Both block hERG/IKr potassium channels; additive QT prolongation
resulting_harm: Torsades de Pointes / sudden cardiac death
notes: Renal impairment and hypokalemia amplify risk significantly

mechanism: Both block hERG potassium channels → additive QT prolongation
resulting_harm: QT prolongation / Torsades de Pointes
notes: Risk elevated in female sex / age >65 / polypharmacy

mechanism: Chronic alcohol induces CYP2E1 → increased toxic NAPQI metabolite formation + depletes glutathione
resulting_harm: Acute hepatotoxicity / cirrhosis / fulminant liver failure
notes: Acute co-ingestion less dangerous than chronic drinkers + APAP; risk highest with regular alcohol + daily acetaminophen use

mechanism: Synergistic GABA potentiation → profound CNS depression
resulting_harm: Severe sedation / respiratory depression / blackouts / overdose death
notes: Synergistic effect disproportionately dangerous; can be fatal at moderate doses of each alone

mechanism: Blocks aldehyde dehydrogenase → acetaldehyde accumulation in blood
resulting_harm: Flushing / nausea / vomiting / chest pain / hypotension / cardiovascular collapse
notes: Intentional deterrent for alcohol use disorder; reaction also triggered by alcohol in mouthwash/sauces/vinegar

mechanism: N-methyltetrazolethiol (N-MTT) side chain inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase → acetaldehyde accumulation
resulting_harm: Disulfiram-like reaction: flushing / nausea / vomiting / hypotension
notes: Only cephalosporins with N-MTT side chain; not a class-wide effect

mechanism: Cocaine + ethanol undergo transesterification → cocaethylene + methanol (via esterases); cocaethylene has longer half-life (~2h vs 1h) and greater cardiotoxicity
resulting_harm: Increased cardiac toxicity / myocardial infarction / cardiomyopathy / sudden cardiac death (18–25x increased mortality vs cocaine alone)
notes: One of the most clinically significant illicit drug-alcohol interactions; produces distinct toxic metabolite

mechanism: Inhibits CYP2C9/CYP2D6/CYP3A4 → warfarin levels increase (0.96 → 1.76 mcg/mL)
resulting_harm: Bleeding risk / hemorrhage
notes: OTC heartburn medicine with serious interaction; patients frequently unaware

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein + reduces renal tubular secretion of digoxin
resulting_harm: Serum digoxin increases 68–800%; clearance reduced 10–44% → digoxin toxicity (arrhythmias / AV block / nausea / visual disturbances)
notes: Serum digoxin nearly doubles; check levels immediately after starting amiodarone

mechanism: Quinidine inhibits renal tubular P-glycoprotein secretion of digoxin
resulting_harm: Digoxin toxicity / arrhythmias / AV block / nausea
notes: Classic interaction; requires digoxin level monitoring when quinidine added

mechanism: Diuretic increases Na+ excretion → kidney compensates by reabsorbing lithium → serum lithium rises 25–40%
resulting_harm: Lithium toxicity: tremor / confusion / seizures / renal damage / arrhythmias
notes: Thiazides have greatest effect on lithium levels; loop diuretics less so

mechanism: NSAIDs decrease prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow → reduced lithium clearance → accumulation
resulting_harm: Lithium toxicity / renal damage
notes: Patients on lithium should avoid NSAIDs; even OTC doses significant

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis → reduced renal perfusion + compete for renal tubular secretion of MTX
resulting_harm: Acute renal failure / MTX accumulation / hepatotoxicity / cytopenia (anemia / leukopenia / thrombocytopenia)
notes: Risk greatest at high MTX doses and pre-existing renal impairment; RA patients often use both

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition in small intestine prevents first-pass metabolism of simvastatin
resulting_harm: Serum levels increase 3.6-fold (260%); myopathy / rhabdomyolysis / acute renal failure
notes: Effect lasts 3–7 days after last grapefruit; applies to atorvastatin and lovastatin; rosuvastatin/pravastatin unaffected

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition → increased CCB bioavailability (felodipine ~5-fold increase)
resulting_harm: Excessive hypotension / reflex tachycardia / syncope / bradycardia (verapamil)
notes: Felodipine was first drug where grapefruit interaction was discovered; clinically relevant at any grapefruit amount

mechanism: Ibuprofen reversibly occupies COX-1 active site and displaces aspirin's irreversible binding — blocks aspirin's antiplatelet mechanism
resulting_harm: Reduced cardioprotective antiplatelet efficacy of aspirin → increased thrombotic event risk
notes: Ibuprofen should be taken 8h after aspirin or 30min before aspirin if both needed; naproxen has less interference
